coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of carbon quantum dots/TiO2/Fe2O3 composites and enhancement of photocatalytic activity under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jinjun
Kuang, Meng
Wang, Jing
Liu, Ruirui
Xie, Shuai
Ji, Zhijiang
coredata.dc:description:
Carbon quantum dots/TiO2/Fe2O3 photocatalytic composite was prepared through multi-step hydrothermal method. The CQDs/TiO2/Fe2O3 composite showed enhanced photocatalytic performance for methylene blue dye photodegration when compared with TiO2/Fe2O3 and pure TiO2 under the visible light irradiation. The mechanism of the improved photocatalytic activity over CQDs/TiO2/Fe2O3 were also investigated. The CQDs can better capture and transfer photogenerated electrons through the TiOC and FeOC bonds. What's more, CQDs can improve the utilization of photogenerated electrons and the electrons in CQDs are captured by O2 to produce
O2 − radicals and the role of
O2 − radicals in the photocatalytic process is significantly improved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
TiO2/Fe2O3
Photocatalysis
Visible light

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of active species and mechanism in the catalytic system

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: The Frontier Exploration Fund Project of China Building Materials Academy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by The Frontier Exploration Fund Project of China Building Materials Academy (No.: YA-567 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM images of C20TF and EDS images; (B) the corresponding elemental mapping of A; (C) and (D) are the TEM images of C20TF.
(A) XRD patterns of different simples; (B) TGA curves of pure TiO2 and CTF with different CQDs content.
XPS spectra of C20TF composites: (A) survey spectra and Ti 2p, (B) Fe 2p, (C) C 1s, (D) O 1s core-level spectra.
FTIR spectra of TiO2, TF and C20TF samples.
(A) UV–Vis DRS spectra of pure TiO2, TF, C10TF and C20TF. (B) PL spectra of TF, C10TF and C20TF.

(A) The degradation curves of MB by photocatalysts and control samples under visible-light irradiation. (B) Kinetic linear simulation curves of MB photocatalytic degradation with photocatalysts under visible-light irradiation. (C) Visible-light spectra of C20TF under visible-light irradiation at different time intervals.

DMPO spin-trapping EPR spectra with the (A)
OH, (B)
O2 −. Free radical experiment for (C) TF and (D) C20TF.
Schematic model for the photocatalytic process of CQDs/TiO2/Fe2O3 composites under visible light.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of carbon quantum dots/TiO2/Fe2O3 composites and enhancement of photocatalytic activity under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China Building Materials Academy, Beijing 100024, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China Building Materials Academy, Beijing 100024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Carbon quantum dots/TiO2/Fe2O3 photocatalytic composite was prepared through multi-step hydrothermal method. The CQDs/TiO2/Fe2O3 composite showed enhanced photocatalytic performance for methylene blue dye photodegration when compared with TiO2/Fe2O3 and pure TiO2 under the visible light irradiation. The mechanism of the improved photocatalytic activity over CQDs/TiO2/Fe2O3 were also investigated. The CQDs can better capture and transfer photogenerated electrons through the TiOC and FeOC bonds. What's more, CQDs can improve the utilization of photogenerated electrons and the electrons in CQDs are captured by O2 to produce
O2 − radicals and the role of
O2 − radicals in the photocatalytic process is significantly improved.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the increasing index of environmental pollution without a systematic solution has become one of the focuses of scientific attention. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been widely used in the field of photocatalysis due to its advantages of low cost, high chemical stability and non-toxicity [1,2]. However, due to the large band gap (anatase, 3.2 eV), TiO2 exhibits high activity only in the ultraviolet region. In order to enhance the effective use of solar energy, many researchers have devoted to the synthesis of TiO2-based photocatalysts with visible light response, including the doping of mental ions [3,4], non-mental ions [5,6] and preparation of composite semiconductors [7,8]. By coupling semiconductors, the photogenerated electrons could be transferred from one semiconductor to another semiconductor to further promote the charge separation. Among these semiconductors, hematite (α-Fe2O3) with low band gap of 2.2 eV could absorb visible light well, and also have advantages of high stability and low cost [9]. The combination of TiO2 with α-Fe2O3 can enhance the photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation, and TiO2/Fe2O3 photocatalyst has been widely used for degradation of organic pollutes and solar water splitting [10–12]. However, the photocatalytic performance of TiO2/Fe2O3 photocatalyst under visible light irradiation still needs to be further improved.

In recent years, carbon materials have been widely used in the research of photocatalytic materials because of their excellent properties, such as graphite [13,14], carbon nanotubes [15] and carbonaceous species [16]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new member of the carbon materials family have already attracted extensive attention from researchers due to its simple preparation, good biocompatibility and photoluminescence [17,18]. Notably, CQDs also possess excellent upconversion photoluminescence property, which is depended on the excitation wavelength and dot size [19,20]. Therefore, photocatalytic materials of CQDs/semiconductor systems are prepared to improve the photocatalytic activity of semiconductors under sunlight, such as Fe2O3/CQDs [21,22], CQDs/Fe2O3@g-C3N4 [23], CQDs/TiO2 [24–26], CQDs/BiOBr [27], TiO2/BiOI/CQDs [28] have been synthesized.

In order to further improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2/Fe2O3 photocatalysts in visible light, CQDs was selected as the sensitizer owing to its favorable electron transport behaviors. Therefore, the CQDs/TiO2/Fe2O3 photocatalysts were prepared through multi-step hydrothermal reaction. The as-prepared samples had a better photocatalytic performance for the initial concentration of methylene blue (MB) solution (100 ml, 20 mg/L) within 180 min under visible light irradiation with respect to TiO2/Fe2O3 photocatalysts. Further, the morphologies, structures, and properties of CQDs/TiO2/Fe2O3 photocatalysts were systematically evaluated by a series of characterization techniques. Finally, the active substances in the photocatalytic process were studied, and the possible mechanism in the catalytic system were proposed.

All the chemicals used in this work were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Tetrabutyl titanate (TBT), acetic acid (HAC, 99.5%), Iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), urea (CH4N2O), citric acid monohydrate (CA) and ethane diamine (EDA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Deionized water was used in all the synthesis processes.

The TNPs were synthesized through a facile solvothermal process [29]. Approximately 68 ml of TBT was added dropwise into 50 ml of HAc solution. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 0.5 h, and then was transferred into a 200 ml Teflonlined stainless steel autoclave maintained at 100 °C for 12 h. These NPs were then collected by centrifugation and thoroughly washed with deionized water and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Finally, the NPs were obtained by annealing in air at 450 °C for 2 h.

The CQDs was prepared according to the literature [30,31]. 6.3042 g of CA and 2.01 ml of EDA were put into 60 ml deionized water. Upon stirring, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a volume of about 200 ml, sealed and kept at 180 °C for 5 h. Then, the mixture was cooled to room temperature and underwent dialysis treatment using a dialysis bag (3500 Da) to remove organic impurities. After filter treatment, the brown solution obtained was kept at 5 °C for use.

2 g pure TNPs, 0.68 g FeCl3·6H2O and 0.4 g urea were dispersed and dissolved in ethanol solution (30 ml C2H5OH, 70 ml H2O). A different amount (0 ml, 10 ml and 20 ml) of CQDs solution was added into the above mixture and kept at 40 °C for 1 h. Then, the mixture solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a volume of about 200 ml, sealed and kept at 180 °C for 24 h. Upon washing, filtering and drying, the final composite products were denoted as TF, C10TF and C20TF, respectively. 10 ml CQDs solution was used to prepare CQDs/TiO2 in the same way, which named as C10/T. And pure TiO2 were also prepared for comparison.

The morphologies elements of samples were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM 3010) and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, EPMA-1720). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was employed to observe the crystal components in the photocatalysts using a Siemens D500 instrument (Cu Kα) between 2θ of 10–80° with a scanning velocity of 2.4 °/min. Thermo gravimetric (TG) were performed using a NETZSCH STA 449 F3 thermal analyzer under atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The XPS analysis was taken by ESCALAB 250 (Thermo, Waltham, MA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a BRUKER TENSOR27 FT-IR instrument with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and 32 scans. The optical properties of the samples were characterized by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian Carry 5000), in which BaSO4 was used as the internal reflectance standard. The PL spectra were obtained at room temperature on a Jasco FP-6500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The active species of the samples were evaluated by EPR.

The liquid-phase photodegradation of methylene blue (MB) was carried out in a cylindrical quartz reactor under the irradiation of visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm) produced by a 300 W Xe arc lamp, and the reactant–light source distance was 20 cm. In a typical experiment, 0.05 g of the as-prepared products was added to 100 ml of MB aqueous solution (20 mg/L). The suspension was stirred for 30 min in the dark to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, the solution was irradiated with a Xe arc lamp and the corresponding solution was taken at certain time intervals for subsequent testing. The absorbance of the corresponding target organics was measured by a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The intensity of the main absorption peak (663 nm) of the MB dye was referred to as a measure of the residual MB dye concentration.

In order to investigate the morphologies and structures of C20TF, SEM and TEM images were provided in Fig. 1 . One can find from Fig. 1A that iron trioxide particles were coated or partially coated with titanium dioxide. The corresponding elemental mapping of Fig. 1A were showed in Fig. 1B, yellow, green 1 For interpretation of color in Fig. 1, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.
1 , blue and red colors represent Ti, O, Fe, C, respectively. The images further showed that Fe and C species were distributed on the surface of TiO2. TEM images of C20TF were showed in Fig. 1C and D. The domain exhibits relatively small TiO2 nanoparticles with a mean size of 12 nm. They possess the anatase phase, with a lattice spacing of 0.347 nm, which agrees well with the (1 0 1) planes of the anatase phase [29,32]. And the crystal lattice spacing around 0.320 nm agrees well with the (0 0 2) spacing of graphite carbon [19,33]. Therefore, the results indicated that the CQDs can be well dispersed on the surface of TiO2, and a good combination were established between the CQDs, TiO2 and Fe2O3.

Fig. 2 A shows the XRD patterns of CTF, CT and CF with different CQDs content. The XRD patterns of the CTF composites clearly show all the diffraction peaks of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No.65-5714) and rhombohedral α-Fe2O3 (JCPDS No.24-0072). The sharp peaks of these samples proved the high crystallinity of the composites. Compared with the diffraction peaks of TF, the intensity of the diffraction peaks of CTF was inhibited. Although the characteristic diffraction peaks of CQDs were not observed in the spectrogram, but combined with TEM results, it was speculated that CQDs inhibited the grain growth of TF composites.

Use TG to determine the amount of CQDs loaded on TF composites, the curves were shown in Fig. 2B, all the curves showed two stages of weight losses. Below 350 °C, the 2.09% weight loss is contributed to the removal of adsorbed water [7,24]. 350 °C later on to 1000 °C, the 1.61% weight loss in pure TiO2 and the 2.28% weight loss in TF were mainly due to the surface-attached water, OH groups and residual organic compound [34]. While more weight loss observed in CTF composites can be ascribed to the combustion of CQDs [26]. Therefore, the content of CQDs in C10TF and C20TF were 0.477% and 1.61%, respectively. In the hydrothermal reaction process, citric acid cannot be completely converted into CQDs, and the excess organic compounds cannot be completely filtered out even after dialysis. In addition, in the process of combining CQDs aqueous solution with TF, as the concentration of CQDs increases in the reaction solution, CQDs increases the probability of the combination of the two by the interaction between surface groups and TF. The above factors will affect the actual compound proportion of CQDs and TF.

XPS analysis was used to characterize the surface chemical composition of C20TF composites, which were shown in Fig. 3 . The full survey spectrum depicts that the composites contain the elements Ti, Fe, O, C. Fig. 3A shows that the binding energies of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 are located at 458.30 eV and 463.95 eV, respectively, which have slightly shifted in comparison to the typical binding energy values of pure TiO2 [35]. It is speculated that new bond structure exists in C20TF composites. As shown in Fig. 3B, the binding energies of Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 were located at 709.81 eV and 723.98 eV, and a weak satellite Fe3+ peak is also observed at 718.36 eV. These peaks are ascribed to 3+ oxidation state of Fe, verifying the formation of α-Fe2O3 stoichiometry [36,37], which is slightly different from those in Fe2O3 (710.7 eV for Fe 2p3/2 and 724.3 eV for Fe 2p1/2) [38]. Since the Fe3+ (60.5 pm) and Ti4+ (64.5 pm) have similar ionic radius, Fe3+ can enter the lattice of TiO2 to form TiOFe bond in reaction. As seen from Fig. 3C, the peaks around 284.32 eV, 285.76 eV and 288.21 eV were associated with CC/CC, CO and CO, respectively [25,26], manifesting the existence of CQDs on the surface of C20TF composites. Fig. 3D shows the high resolution O 1s spectrum, where three peaks at 529.35 eV, 530.72 eV and 531.66 eV can be attributed to TiO & FeO, CO & OCO and H2O related bonds, respectively [12,36,39], indicating the formation of TiOC & FeOC bonds on the surface of C20TF composites (the binding energies of Ti 2p and Fe 2p are both have slightly shifted). The XPS analysis indicates that the stable combination of CQDs, TiO2 and α-Fe2O3 has been constructed in C20TF composites.

FTIR spectra of TiO2, TF and C20TF samples are shown in Fig. 4 . The samples show FTIR bands at 3418 cm−1 corresponding to OH, at 1629 cm−1 are attributed to bending vibration of adsorbed water molecules. The peaks at 2928 cm−1, 2845 cm−1, 1502 cm−1, 1421 cm-1and 1364 cm−1 are assigned to sp3 and sp2 CH, CO, unsaturated CH and COH bonds in curve C20TF [25,31,40], indicating the existence of CQDs in the composites. In addition, the broad absorption band between 826 cm−1 and 482 cm−1 corresponding to TiO stretching and TF sample shows band at 549 cm−1 which are the characteristic of Fe2O3 [12]. What's more, a slightly shift of the peak of FeO at 570 cm−1 can be observed in C20TF sample, suggesting the crystal structure of Fe2O3 were distorted and FeOC bonds were formed in C20TF composites, which was in agreement with the XPS results.

Fig. 5 A displays the UV–Vis DRS date. Pure TiO2 only absorbed UV radiation of less than 400 nm. The spectra of TF composite displayed a red shift in the band gap transition. This resulted from Fe3+ was doped with the TiO2 lattice during the synthesis of the composite [41,42]. Furthermore, the absorbance intensity of CTF samples are higher than that of pure TiO2 and TF in the whole visible region. The C20TF sample exhibited not only stronger absorption in the UV region of less than 400 nm, but also adequate and strong absorption in the visible light region of 400–600 nm. Therefore, CQDs play a critical role in the enhanced visible light absorption of the CTF composite, which is expected to enhance its visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity.

The PL spectra of Pure TiO2, TF, C10TF and C20TF samples excited by light with wavelength of 375 nm are shown in Fig. 5B. As it is well known, the PL were used to evaluate the transfer and recombination process of the photoexcited electron-hole pairs, and the lower the PL spectra intensity, the higher the efficiency of separation and transfer of electron-hole pairs [42]. Compared with pure TiO2 and TiO2/Fe2O3, the CTF composites show lower PL intensity, revealing that the CQDs on the surface of TiO2/Fe2O3 plays a role of electron capture, which can promote the effective separation of photo-generated carriers.

The photocatalytic properties of the prepared photocatalysts under visible light (WL > 425 nm) were evaluated, MB was selected as a model pollutant, and the results of photocatalytic degradation experiments are shown in Fig. 6 . Prior to the dye photodegradation experiments, dark adsorption were carried out for 30 min to eliminate the influence of adsorption on the degradation process. It can be observed that the photo-induced dye degradation efficiency is up to 86.5% in 180 min with C20TF as the photocatalyst. For comparison, pure TiO2, TF and C10TF were used as photocatalysts in the contrast experiments, and the activity of all photocatalysts is in the following order: C20TF > C10TF > TF > pure TiO2. Kinetics of photocatalytic degradation of MB by CTF composites were investigated using Langmuir-Hinshelwood dynamic model (Fig. 6B) [7,26]. The result showed that the photocatalysis kinetics follows pseudo-first-order rule, C20TF sample shows the highest k value (0.01105 m−1), was 5 times than that of TF (k = 0.00238 m−1), and was 50 times than that of pure TiO2 (k = 0.00028 m−1). It indicates that the photocatalysts composed of three materials, Fe2O3, CQDs and TiO2, have better photocatalytic properties, and the addition of CQDs significantly improves the utilization efficiency of TiO2/Fe2O3 to visible light. This result is consistent with the previous UV–Vis DSC and PL analysis results. To further study the degradation process, Fig. 6C showed the absorbance spectra of MB degraded by C20TF at different time under visible light. The peaks at 609 nm and 663 nm correspond to the characteristic absorption peaks of MB. The peaks intensity gradually decreases with the extension of illumination time, and the peak attenuation at the wavelength of 609 nm were faster. The results also confirmed that MB molecules were gradually decomposed and the structure of the reaction products had changed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of active species and mechanism in the catalytic system
ce:section.ce:para:
The active substances in the photocatalytic process were studied by using electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technology and DMPO as the trapping agent [43]. As seen from Fig. 7 A, under visible light irradiation, more active species of
OH were generated on the surface of TiO2/Fe2O3 and TiO2/Fe2O3 modified by CQDs. The
O2 − active species (Fig. 7B) generated have the similar results with the
OH active species. It can be seen that both
OH and
O2 − play important roles in the photocatalytic reaction of CQDs/TiO2/Fe2O3 composites. To gain a more detailed insight into the effect of active species on photocatalytic processes, tert-butyl alcohol (TBA, 1 mmol/L), p-Benzoquinone (p-BQ, 1 mmol/L) and ammonium oxalate (AO, 1 mmol/L) were used as scavengers for
OH,
O2 − and h+, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7C and D, the addition of TBA significantly inhibited the photocatalytic efficiency of both TF and CTF catalysts, indicating that
OH plays an important role in the photocatalytic process. For the addition of p-BQ, the photocatalytic effect on TF was very small, but the inhibition of the photocatalytic effect of CTF was relatively obvious. This phenomenon suggests that the addition of CQDs enhances the transmission and utilization of photogenerated electrons, and converts the active oxygen adsorbed on the surface into
O2 − with strong oxidizing property. Therefore,
O2 − played an active role in the photocatalytic process of CTF. By comparing the photocatalytic efficiency of TF and CTF after AO addition, it can be seen that the effect of h+ in the photocatalytic process of CTF was significantly weaker than that of TF. Combined with the previous analysis, it can be inferred that
O2 − was converted to
OH in this photocatalysis process.

As we have known, TiO2 has no absorption response to the visible light due to its wide band gap, the light absorption of TiO2/Fe2O3 was solely contributed by the Fe2O3 in the visible light photocatalysis progress. The schematic diagram of photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/TiO2/Fe2O3 composite is shown in Scheme 1 . When TiO2 and Fe2O3 particles were coupled with each other, the Fermi level of TiO2 and Fe2O3 must align in equilibrium due to the presence of the Fe2O3/TiO2 heterojunction [41,44,45]. Therefore, under the light irradiation, the photogenerated electrons (e-) in Fe2O3 are excited from valence band (VB) to conduction band (CB) and transferred to CB of TiO2. CQDs, as the carbon nanostructure, has a huge capacity of electron storage [17]. According to the different positions of CQDs in the composite structure, different charge transfer modes are caused. The electrons in CQDs are captured by O2 to produce
O2 − radicals. At the same time, the holes (h+) oxidize H2O and OH to hydroxyl radical (
OH). The
O2 − and
OH radicals both have very strong oxidation capability to degrade MO dye. Moreover, the π-π bonds between the CQDs and MB (the benzene ring structure) is beneficial to adsorb MB molecules on the CQDs/TiO2/Fe2O3 photocatalysts surface [21,30,46]. These processes cooperative effects are together responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2/Fe2O3 photocatalysts.

In summary, CQDs/TiO2/Fe2O3 photocatalytic composites were prepared via multi-step hydrothermal reaction. The CTF composites showed enhanced photocatalytic performance for MB dye photodegration when compared with TiO2/Fe2O3 and pure TiO2 under the visible light irradiation. According to the analysis and discussion in the paper, CQDs can better capture and transfer photogenerated electrons through the TiOC and FeOC bonds formed between the CQDs and TiO2/Fe2O3, effectively separate electrons and holes. What's more, CQDs can improve the utilization of photogenerated electrons and the introduction of CQDs enhances the role of
O2 − radicals in the photocatalytic process of CQDs/TiO2/Fe2O3 composites.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by The Frontier Exploration Fund Project of China Building Materials Academy (No.: YA-567).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A low-cost, high-efficiency solar cell based on dye-sensitized colloidal TiO2 films

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O'Regan, B. and M. Grätzel, A low-cost, high-efficiency solar cell based on dye-sensitized colloidal TiO2 films. Nature, 1991. 353(6346): p. 737-740.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heterostructures construction on TiO2 nanobelts: A powerful tool for building high-performance photocatalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, X., et al., Heterostructures construction on TiO2 nanobelts: A powerful tool for building high-performance photocatalysts. Applied Catalysis B: Environmental, 2017. 202: p. 620-641.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pd/TiO2 doped faujasite photocatalysts for acetophenone transfer hydrogenation in a photocatalytic membrane reactor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lavorato, C., et al., Pd/TiO2 doped faujasite photocatalysts for acetophenone transfer hydrogenation in a photocatalytic membrane reactor. Journal of Catalysis, 2017. 353: p. 152-161.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoinduced electron storage and surface plasmon modulation in Ag@TiO2 clusters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hirakawa, T. and P.V. Kamat, Photoinduced Electron Storage and Surface Plasmon Modulation in Ag@TiO2 Clusters. Langmuir, 2004. 20(14): p. 5645-5647.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of N-doped TiO2/C nanocomposites with high visible light photocatalytic activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, D., et al., One-step hydrothermal synthesis of N-doped TiO2/C nanocomposites with high visible light photocatalytic activity. Nanoscale, 2012. 4(2): p. 576-584.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel electroless plating of Ni–P–TiO2 nano-composite coatings

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, W., W. Gao and Y. He, A novel electroless plating of Ni–P–TiO2 nano-composite coatings. Surface and Coatings Technology, 2010. 204(15): p. 2493-2498.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile one-step synthesis of TiO2/Ag/SnO2 ternary heterostructures with enhanced visible light photocatalytic activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, Z., et al., Facile one-step synthesis of TiO2/Ag/SnO2 ternary heterostructures with enhanced visible light photocatalytic activity. Scientific Reports, 2018. 8(1): p. 1-11.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of one-dimensional TiO2/V2O5 branched heterostructures and their visible light photocatalytic activity towards Rhodamine B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, Y., et al., Synthesis of one-dimensional TiO2/V2O5 branched heterostructures and their visible light photocatalytic activity towards Rhodamine B. Nanotechnology, 2011. 22(22): p. 225702.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of novel a-Fe2O3 nanorods without surfactant and its electrochemical performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bazrafshan, H., et al., Synthesis of novel α-Fe2O3 nanorods without surfactant and its electrochemical performance. Powder Technology, 2017. 308: p. 266-272.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and characterization of visible light responsive Fe2O3–TiO2 composites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu, H., et al., Preparation and characterization of visible light responsive Fe2O3–TiO2 composites. Applied Surface Science, 2011. 257(13): p. 5813-5819.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoanodes based on TiO2 and a-Fe2O3 for solar water splitting - superior role of 1D nanoarchitectures and of combined heterostructures

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kment, S., et al., Photoanodes based on TiO2 and α-Fe2O3 for solar water splitting - superior role of 1D nanoarchitectures and of combined heterostructures. Chemical Society Reviews, 2017. 46(12): p.3716-3769.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of TiO2@ a-Fe2O3 core–shell heteronanostructures by thermal decomposition approach and their application towards sunlight-driven photodegradation of rhodamine B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mahajan, J. and P. Jeevanandam, Synthesis of TiO2@ α-Fe2O3 core–shell heteronanostructures by thermal decomposition approach and their application towards sunlight-driven photodegradation of rhodamine B. New Journal of Chemistry, 2018. 42(4): p. 2616-2626.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent developments of graphene-TiO2 composite nanomaterials as efficient photoelectrodes in dye-sensitized solar cells: A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Low, F.W. and C.W. Lai, Recent developments of graphene-TiO2 composite nanomaterials as efficient photoelectrodes in dye-sensitized solar cells: A review. Renewable and Sustainable Energy Reviews, 2018. 82: p. 103-125.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of few-layer MoS2 nanosheets-coated TiO2 nanosheets on graphite fibers for enhanced photocatalytic properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hu, X., et al., Synthesis of few-layer MoS2 nanosheets-coated TiO2 nanosheets on graphite fibers for enhanced photocatalytic properties. Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells, 2017. 172: p. 108-116.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biomineralized N-doped CNT/TiO2 core/shell nanowires for visible light photocatalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lee, W.J., et al., Biomineralized N-Doped CNT/TiO2 Core/Shell Nanowires for Visible Light Photocatalysis. ACS Nano, 2011. 6(1): p. 935-943.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis and characterization of N-doped TiO2/C nanocomposites with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jia, T., et al., Facile synthesis and characterization of N-doped TiO2/C nanocomposites with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic performance. Applied Surface Science, 2018. 430: p. 438-447.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lim, S.Y., W. Shen and Z. Gao, Carbon quantum dots and their applications. Chemical Society Reviews, 2015. 44(1): p. 362-381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots from natural resource: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Das, R., R. Bandyopadhyay and P. Pramanik, Carbon quantum dots from natural resource: A review. Materials Today Chemistry, 2018. 8: p. 96-109.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, H., et al., Water-Soluble Fluorescent Carbon Quantum Dots and Photocatalyst Design. Angewandte Chemie International Edition, 2010. 49(26): p. 4430-4434.
